enrichment.short_description: Ory is an open-source identity and access management platform providing authentication, authorization, and fine-grained permissions infrastructure, available self-hosted or as the managed Ory Network cloud service.
enrichment.seo_title: Ory Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ory review 2026: open-source identity, authentication and permissions infrastructure. Compare Ory Network pricing, features, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Ory is an open-source identity and access management (IAM) infrastructure company founded in 2015 by Aeneas Rekkas, Simon Kohl, and Thomas Aidan Curran, and headquartered in Munich, Germany, with a distributed team. What began as an open-source project grew into Ory Corp, which raised a Series A round of $22 million led by Insight Partners (bringing total funding to roughly $27.5 million) to build out its commercial managed offering, Ory Network. Ory's product suite is modular: Ory Kratos handles identity management, registration, login, multi-factor authentication and account recovery; Ory Hydra is a certified OAuth2 and OpenID Connect server; Ory Keto is a permissions and authorization engine modeled on Google's Zanzibar paper for fine-grained access control at scale; and Ory Oathkeeper acts as an identity and access proxy for zero-trust API gateways. All core components are open source under the Apache 2.0 license and can be self-hosted independently or combined, which differentiates Ory from monolithic, closed-source identity providers. For teams that do not want to operate this infrastructure themselves, Ory Network offers the same components as a fully managed cloud service. Pricing follows a freemium, usage-based model: a free Developer tier supports evaluation and prototyping with two development environments and community support; the Production plan starts at $70/month and covers one production project, three staging environments, and five development projects, with usage-based add-ons for machine-to-machine tokens and permission checks; the Growth plan starts at $9,350/year and adds multitenancy support, more environments, and lower per-unit usage pricing; and an Enterprise plan offers custom contracts for large organizations with compliance and support requirements. Ory targets software engineering and platform teams building consumer or B2B products that need login, SSO, MFA, and fine-grained authorization without building identity infrastructure from scratch, ranging from early-stage startups self-hosting the open-source components to scale-ups and enterprises on the managed Ory Network. Its main competitors include Auth0 (part of Okta), Keycloak (the dominant open-source alternative), FusionAuth, WorkOS, and cloud-native options like AWS Cognito, with Ory differentiating itself through its API-first, headless architecture, Zanzibar-style permissions engine, and open-source-first licensing that avoids vendor lock-in.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Ory is an open-source identity and access management platform built around a modular set of components: Kratos for identity, Hydra for OAuth2/OIDC, Keto for permissions, and Oathkeeper for API access control. Each piece can be run independently, self-hosted, or consumed together through the managed Ory Network cloud service.

Founded in 2015 in Munich, Germany, Ory grew from an open-source project into a venture-backed company after raising a $22 million Series A led by Insight Partners. The company positions itself as infrastructure for developers who want authentication and authorization without building it from scratch or locking into a closed-source vendor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Ory Kratos provides self-service registration, login, account recovery, and multi-factor and passwordless authentication as headless, API-first identity management. Ory Hydra is a certified OAuth2 and OpenID Connect server for building SSO and API authorization flows.

Ory Keto implements a Zanzibar-inspired permissions engine for fine-grained authorization at scale, and Ory Oathkeeper acts as an identity-aware proxy for zero-trust API gateways. All four components are open source under Apache 2.0 and can be deployed self-hosted or via the managed Ory Network.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Ory's free Developer tier is intended for evaluation and prototyping, with two development environments and community-only support, and the open-source components can always be self-hosted for free.

Paid Ory Network plans scale from Production at $70/month (one production project, usage-based token and permission-check pricing) to Growth at $9,350/year for larger, multi-tenant deployments, up to custom Enterprise pricing for large organizations with compliance needs.

features.title: Ory Kratos identity management
features.description: Headless, API-first user registration, login, MFA, and account recovery.

features.title: Ory Hydra OAuth2/OIDC server
features.description: Certified OAuth2 and OpenID Connect provider for building SSO and delegated authorization.

features.title: Ory Keto permissions engine
features.description: Fine-grained, Zanzibar-inspired authorization for modeling complex permission relationships.

features.title: Ory Oathkeeper access proxy
features.description: Identity and access proxy for enforcing authentication and authorization at the API gateway layer.

features.title: Ory Network managed cloud
features.description: Fully managed hosting of the Ory stack for teams that do not want to self-host.

features.title: Passwordless and social sign-on
features.description: Support for passkeys, magic links, and social login providers alongside traditional credentials.

features.title: Multi-language SDKs
features.description: Client SDKs and API references for integrating Ory into apps across major programming languages and frameworks.

pros: Fully open-source core components under the permissive Apache 2.0 license
Modular architecture lets teams adopt only the pieces they need (identity, OAuth2, permissions, or gateway)
Zanzibar-style permissions engine (Keto) handles complex authorization models that many competitors lack
Both self-hosted and fully managed (Ory Network) deployment options available
API-first, headless design integrates cleanly into custom frontends and mobile apps
cons: Self-hosting the full stack requires meaningful DevOps and identity domain expertise
Documentation and configuration can be complex for teams new to headless identity infrastructure
Usage-based pricing on Ory Network can grow significantly with active user and permission-check volume
Higher-tier features like multitenancy are gated behind the more expensive Growth and Enterprise plans
Smaller ecosystem and community size compared to more established players like Keycloak

faqs.question: Is Ory free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ory's core components (Kratos, Hydra, Keto, Oathkeeper) are open source under the Apache 2.0 license and free to self-host. The managed Ory Network also offers a free Developer tier for evaluation and prototyping.

faqs.question: What is Ory Kratos?
faqs.answer: Ory Kratos is Ory's identity management component, handling user registration, login, multi-factor authentication, and account recovery through a headless, API-first architecture.

faqs.question: What is the difference between self-hosting Ory and using Ory Network?
faqs.answer: Self-hosting means running the open-source Ory components (Kratos, Hydra, Keto, Oathkeeper) on your own infrastructure for free. Ory Network is Ory's fully managed cloud version of the same components, priced on a freemium, usage-based model starting at $70/month for production use.

faqs.question: How much does Ory Network cost?
faqs.answer: Ory Network has a free Developer tier, a Production plan starting at $70/month, a Growth plan starting at $9,350/year, and custom Enterprise pricing, with usage-based charges for items like machine-to-machine tokens and permission checks.

faqs.question: Does Ory support passwordless authentication?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ory supports passwordless and passkey-based authentication, along with social sign-on and traditional password and multi-factor authentication flows.

faqs.question: What is Ory Keto used for?
faqs.answer: Ory Keto is a fine-grained permissions and authorization engine modeled on Google's Zanzibar paper, used to model complex access-control relationships between users, groups, and resources.

faqs.question: Who are Ory's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Ory's main competitors include Auth0 (owned by Okta), Keycloak, FusionAuth, WorkOS, and AWS Cognito.

faqs.question: Is Ory suitable for enterprise use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ory offers a custom Enterprise plan on Ory Network with dedicated support and compliance features, in addition to its open-source components that enterprises can self-host under their own security controls.

use_cases.title: Consumer app authentication
use_cases.description: Product teams use Ory Kratos and Hydra to add login, MFA, and social sign-on to consumer-facing web and mobile apps without building identity infrastructure in-house.
use_cases.audience: Product and engineering teams at startups and scale-ups

use_cases.title: B2B SaaS authorization and multitenancy
use_cases.description: B2B SaaS companies use Ory Keto's Zanzibar-style permissions engine to model complex, multi-tenant role and resource permissions.

use_cases.title: Zero-trust API access control
use_cases.description: Platform and security teams deploy Ory Oathkeeper as an identity-aware proxy in front of internal and external APIs to enforce zero-trust access policies.

features: Full access to core identity and permissions APIs for evaluation
Usage-based machine-to-machine tokens and permission checks

enrichment.short_description: osem (Open Source Event Manager) is a free, self-hosted, Ruby on Rails event management platform built by the openSUSE community for running Free and Open Source Software conferences, from call-for-papers through scheduling.
enrichment.seo_title: osem Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: osem review 2026: the free, open-source event manager built for FOSS conferences. Compare features, self-hosting setup and alternatives like Pretalx.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: osem, short for Open Source Event Manager, is a free and open-source event management platform maintained by the openSUSE community and built on Ruby on Rails. It is purpose-built for organizing Free and Open Source Software conferences rather than general commercial events, and its GitHub repository (openSUSE/osem) is MIT licensed with thousands of commits and hundreds of forks, reflecting a long-running community-maintained codebase. osem covers the full lifecycle of running a technical conference: organizers can open a call for papers, allow speakers to submit and edit proposals, classify and evaluate submissions through a review workflow, build and publish an event schedule and program page, manage speaker and volunteer information, and let attendees register and, in some configurations, purchase tickets. The platform has been used in production to run multiple editions of the openSUSE Conference and the ownCloud Conference, with organizers citing events with over 1,000 attendees and 500 paper submissions handled through the tool, demonstrating it works at real conference scale despite being a community-run project rather than a funded startup. There is no commercial company behind osem and no pricing tiers: it is entirely free, distributed as source code that organizers self-host, commonly via Docker and Docker Compose, on their own servers. Because it is a community project rather than a company, there is no official managed hosting or paid support tier, and organizations adopting it need someone comfortable running a Rails application in production. osem's target users are FOSS conference organizers, user-group and meetup organizers, and open-source foundations that want a purpose-built call-for-papers and scheduling tool rather than adapting a generic event-ticketing SaaS product to conference needs. Its closest alternatives include Pretalx (another open-source conference management tool popular in the Python and open-source event circuit), Sessionize, Indico (widely used in scientific and academic conference settings), and general-purpose commercial platforms like Eventbrite or Cvent, though those commercial tools lack osem's specific call-for-papers and program-review workflow built for volunteer-run technical conferences. osem's main differentiators are that it is free, fully open source, purpose-built for FOSS/tech conferences, and proven across multiple real-world community conferences over more than a decade of use.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: osem, or Open Source Event Manager, is a free, open-source event management tool maintained by the openSUSE community and built on Ruby on Rails. It was created specifically to handle the workflow of running Free and Open Source Software conferences, rather than general commercial events.

The tool has been used to run multiple editions of the openSUSE Conference and the ownCloud Conference, handling events with over 1,000 attendees and 500 paper submissions, and remains actively developed as a community open-source project on GitHub under the MIT license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: osem supports the full conference lifecycle: opening a call for papers, collecting and reviewing speaker submissions, building and publishing an event schedule, and managing registration. Organizers can create a public event website and splash page directly from the platform.

Because it targets volunteer-run technical conferences specifically, osem includes program-review and classification workflows for evaluating paper submissions that many general-purpose event platforms lack, along with speaker and volunteer coordination tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: osem is completely free and open source with no paid tiers, since it is maintained by the openSUSE community rather than a commercial company.
Organizers self-host osem on their own infrastructure, typically using Docker, and there is no official managed hosting or paid support offering; the only cost is the organizer's own server hosting.

features.title: Call for papers management
features.description: Open a public call for papers and let speakers submit, edit, and track proposals for a conference.

features.title: Submission review and classification
features.description: Organizers evaluate, score, and classify incoming paper submissions through a structured review workflow.

features.title: Event schedule and program builder
features.description: Build and publish a detailed conference schedule with session, room, and time information.

features.title: Event website and splash pages
features.description: Generate a public-facing event page directly from the platform for marketing and information.

features.title: Attendee registration
features.description: Let visitors register for the event, with support for ticketing in some deployments.

features.title: Speaker and volunteer management
features.description: Track speaker details and coordinate volunteers involved in running the conference.

features.title: Self-hosted Docker deployment
features.description: Deploy osem on your own infrastructure using Docker and Docker Compose.

pros: Completely free and open source with the MIT license
Purpose-built for the specific workflow of FOSS and technical conferences
Proven in production across multiple real conferences with over 1,000 attendees
Long-running, community-maintained project with an active GitHub repository
Full control over data and hosting since it is entirely self-hosted
cons: Requires self-hosting and Ruby on Rails operational knowledge, unlike turnkey SaaS event tools
No official managed hosting or paid support tier available
User interface is more utilitarian than modern commercial event platforms
Smaller contributor community than mainstream event-management SaaS products
Best suited to technical/FOSS conferences rather than general commercial events

faqs.question: Is osem free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, osem is entirely free and open source under the MIT license, maintained by the openSUSE community with no paid tiers or licensing fees.

faqs.question: Who maintains osem?
faqs.answer: osem is maintained by the openSUSE community as an open-source project on GitHub, rather than by a commercial company.

faqs.question: What is osem used for?
faqs.answer: osem is used to run Free and Open Source Software conferences, covering call-for-papers submission, review, scheduling, event websites, and attendee registration.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host osem?
faqs.answer: Yes, osem is a self-hosted platform. Organizers deploy it on their own servers, commonly using Docker, since there is no official managed cloud version.

faqs.question: What technology is osem built with?
faqs.answer: osem is built primarily with Ruby on Rails, with additional use of Haml for templating and Docker for deployment.

faqs.question: Which conferences have used osem?
faqs.answer: osem has powered multiple editions of the openSUSE Conference and the ownCloud Conference, among other Free and Open Source Software community events.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to osem?
faqs.answer: Alternatives include Pretalx, Indico, Sessionize, and general-purpose commercial event platforms like Eventbrite, though those lack osem's conference-specific call-for-papers review workflow.

use_cases.title: FOSS conference call for papers
use_cases.description: Conference organizers use osem to open a public call for papers, collect speaker submissions, and run a structured review process.

use_cases.title: Technical conference scheduling
use_cases.description: Organizers build and publish multi-track conference schedules and session details directly through osem's program builder.

use_cases.title: Community event registration
use_cases.description: User groups and open-source foundations use osem to manage attendee registration for community-run technical events.

enrichment.short_description: osquery is an open-source, SQL-powered operating system instrumentation and monitoring framework, originally created by Facebook in 2014 and now governed by the vendor-neutral OSquery Foundation under the Linux Foundation.
enrichment.company_size: Not publicly disclosed (governed by the OSquery Foundation, a Linux Foundation project)
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, United States (Linux Foundation)
enrichment.seo_title: osquery Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: osquery review 2026: the open-source SQL framework for OS instrumentation and security monitoring. Features, governance, pricing and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: osquery is an open-source operating system instrumentation and monitoring framework that exposes an operating system's state, including processes, users, installed software, network connections, and hardware, as a set of relational database tables that can be queried with standard SQL. It was created by Facebook and publicly released as open source on October 29, 2014, initially supporting Linux and macOS, with full Windows support added in late 2016. The tool quickly saw adoption at major technology companies including Airbnb, Dropbox, Netflix, Etsy, Uber, and Palantir, who used it for security monitoring, incident response, and IT asset visibility at scale. Following community concerns that Facebook was not sufficiently investing in the project's long-term stewardship, Facebook and the Linux Foundation jointly announced in June 2019 the intent to form a new, vendor-neutral home for osquery; the OSquery Foundation was subsequently established under the Linux Foundation, governed by a Technical Steering Committee with engineers from companies including Meta, Trail of Bits, Google, Kolide, and Uptycs. osquery's core architecture centers on two tools: osqueryi, an interactive SQL shell for ad hoc queries and exploration, and osqueryd, a low-footprint daemon that schedules queries across a fleet of hosts and aggregates results into logs that flag state changes over time. The osquery schema spans more than 280 tables covering hardware, software, and OS events, and the project is dual-licensed under Apache 2.0 and GPLv2. Because osquery itself is a query engine rather than a full fleet-management platform, organizations typically pair it with a management layer; the most common are Fleet (an open-source osquery management platform) and Kolide (a commercial device-trust platform built on osquery), both of which add dashboards, deployment tooling, and alerting on top of the core project. osquery is entirely free and open source with no paid tiers of its own; commercial spending in this space goes toward these third-party fleet-management and endpoint-security layers rather than to osquery itself. Its target users are security engineers, detection and response teams, and IT operations teams needing deep, queryable visibility into endpoint and server state across large, heterogeneous fleets. Its closest points of comparison include Wazuh (an open-source SIEM/EDR platform), Fleet and Kolide (osquery-based fleet management), and commercial EDR products like CrowdStrike Falcon and SentinelOne, though osquery differentiates itself as a lightweight, vendor-neutral, SQL-first instrumentation layer rather than a full managed security product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: osquery is an open-source framework that turns an operating system into a queryable relational database, letting security and IT teams run SQL queries against low-level system state such as running processes, installed packages, users, and network connections. It was originally built and open-sourced by Facebook in 2014.

In 2019, governance of the project moved from Facebook to the newly formed OSquery Foundation under the Linux Foundation, making it a vendor-neutral, community-governed project overseen by a Technical Steering Committee with representatives from companies including Meta, Trail of Bits, Google, and Kolide.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: osquery ships with two primary tools: osqueryi, an interactive SQL shell for exploring system state, and osqueryd, a lightweight daemon that runs scheduled queries across a fleet of machines and logs state changes over time. Its schema exposes more than 280 tables covering hardware, software, and OS-level events.

The project supports Windows, macOS, and major Linux distributions, and is extensible through custom tables and extensions. Because osquery itself does not include a management dashboard, it is commonly deployed alongside a fleet-management layer such as Fleet or Kolide.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: osquery itself is completely free and open source, dual-licensed under Apache 2.0 and GPLv2, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or licensing fees for the core project.

Organizations that want a management UI, alerting, or fleet deployment tooling on top of osquery typically adopt a separate product, such as the open-source Fleet platform or the commercial Kolide device-trust platform, which carry their own independent pricing.

features.title: SQL-based system querying
features.description: Query operating system state, such as processes, users, and network connections, using standard SQL through the osqueryi shell.

features.title: osqueryd scheduled monitoring daemon
features.description: A low-footprint daemon that runs scheduled queries across a fleet and logs state changes over time.

features.title: 280+ table schema
features.description: A relational schema covering hardware, software, and operating system events across supported platforms.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Runs on Windows, macOS, and major Linux distributions including Ubuntu and CentOS.

features.title: Extensible via custom tables
features.description: Teams can build custom tables and extensions to expose additional system or application data.

features.title: Vendor-neutral open governance
features.description: Governed by the OSquery Foundation under the Linux Foundation with a multi-company Technical Steering Committee.

features.title: Dual open-source licensing
features.description: Distributed under both Apache 2.0 and GPLv2 licenses.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing costs
Powerful SQL abstraction makes complex endpoint investigation queries accessible to teams already comfortable with SQL
Cross-platform support across Windows, macOS, and Linux
Vendor-neutral governance under the Linux Foundation reduces single-company risk
Proven at scale by major technology companies including Airbnb, Netflix, and Uber
Extensible schema supports custom tables for organization-specific data
cons: No built-in fleet management UI, dashboards, or alerting out of the box
Requires pairing with a separate tool like Fleet or Kolide for practical fleet-wide deployment
Writing effective, performant queries at scale requires a learning curve
Log pipeline and alerting infrastructure must be built or integrated separately
Community development pace can vary since it depends on volunteer and sponsoring-company contributions

faqs.question: Is osquery free?
faqs.answer: Yes, osquery is completely free and open source, dual-licensed under Apache 2.0 and GPLv2, with no paid tiers for the core project itself.

faqs.question: Who created osquery?
faqs.answer: osquery was created by Facebook and publicly released as open source on October 29, 2014. Governance later transferred to the vendor-neutral OSquery Foundation under the Linux Foundation in 2019.

faqs.question: What is the difference between osqueryi and osqueryd?
faqs.answer: osqueryi is an interactive SQL shell for running ad hoc queries against system state, while osqueryd is a background daemon that runs scheduled queries across a fleet of machines and logs changes over time.

faqs.question: Does osquery include a management dashboard?
faqs.answer: No, osquery itself is a query engine and daemon without a built-in fleet dashboard. Most organizations pair it with a separate tool like Fleet or Kolide for fleet-wide management and alerting.

faqs.question: What platforms does osquery support?
faqs.answer: osquery supports Windows, macOS, and major Linux distributions such as Ubuntu and CentOS, with a schema of over 280 tables covering hardware, software, and OS events.

faqs.question: Who governs osquery today?
faqs.answer: osquery is governed by the OSquery Foundation, a project under the Linux Foundation, directed by a Technical Steering Committee with members from companies including Meta, Trail of Bits, Google, and Kolide.

faqs.question: What companies use osquery?
faqs.answer: osquery has been used in production by companies including Airbnb, Dropbox, Netflix, Etsy, Uber, and Palantir for security monitoring and infrastructure visibility.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to osquery?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives and complements include Wazuh, Fleet, Kolide, and commercial EDR platforms like CrowdStrike Falcon and SentinelOne.

use_cases.title: Security incident investigation
use_cases.description: Security teams use osquery's SQL interface to investigate endpoint compromises by querying process, network, and file system state across affected hosts.

use_cases.title: Fleet-wide compliance monitoring
use_cases.description: IT and security teams schedule osqueryd queries across their device fleet to continuously monitor for configuration drift and compliance violations.

use_cases.title: Infrastructure visibility at scale
use_cases.description: DevOps teams at large technology companies use osquery to gain queryable visibility into server and workstation state across thousands of hosts.

enrichment.short_description: Open Source Social Network (OSSN) is a free, self-hosted PHP script for building custom social networking websites and online communities, with an optional one-time-purchase premium version adding extra features.
enrichment.seo_title: OSSN Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OSSN review 2026: the free, self-hosted PHP social network script. Compare core and premium features, one-time pricing and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OSSN, or Open Source Social Network, is a free, self-hosted PHP script that lets developers and organizations build custom social networking websites, online communities, membership platforms, and private collaboration spaces without relying on a hosted third-party social media provider. The project was founded in 2014, with its GitHub organization maintained under opensource-socialnetwork, and it functions similarly in spirit to open-source Facebook alternatives, giving site owners full ownership of their codebase, branding, and user data. Out of the box, OSSN provides the core building blocks of a social network: user profiles, chronological timelines, groups, private messaging, photo albums, real-time likes and comments, notifications, and basic content sharing, all built on a hooks-and-events architecture that lets developers extend functionality with custom plugins and themes without modifying core files. Because it is self-hosted PHP software, site owners install it on their own server or hosting provider (it is also distributed through marketplaces like DigitalOcean's one-click app catalog and AWS Marketplace), giving them full data sovereignty, no per-user licensing caps, and no dependency on a third-party platform that could change its terms of service or shut down. OSSN's pricing model is freemium in the loosest sense: the core script is entirely free and open source with no subscription fees, while a separate premium version is sold for a one-time fee of $199 (lifetime license) that adds features such as hashtags, native video support, polls, events, stories, and link previews, features increasingly expected on modern social platforms but not included in the free core. There is no recurring SaaS subscription for either tier. OSSN's target users are developers and small organizations building niche community sites, enterprise intranets, alumni or membership networks, and privacy-focused social platforms who want full control over their social network's code and data rather than building on a hosted network like Facebook Groups or Mighty Networks. Its closest alternatives include Mastodon and other ActivityPub-based federated social platforms, Elgg (another long-running open-source social networking framework), BuddyPress (a WordPress-based social networking plugin), and hosted community platforms like Mighty Networks or Circle, with OSSN differentiating itself through its low one-time cost for premium features, PHP-based simplicity for developers already familiar with that ecosystem, and its explicit positioning as a full self-hosted alternative to commercial hosted social networks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OSSN, or Open Source Social Network, is a free, self-hosted PHP script for building custom social networking websites and online communities. Founded in 2014, the project provides the core infrastructure of a social platform, including profiles, timelines, groups, and messaging, as free and open-source software that site owners install and control themselves.

Because it is self-hosted, OSSN gives organizations full ownership of their codebase, branding, and user data, avoiding the vendor lock-in and data-control concerns that come with building a community on a third-party hosted social network.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The free core includes user profiles, chronological timelines, groups, forums, private messaging, photo albums, real-time likes and comments, and notifications, all built on an extensible hooks-and-events architecture that supports custom themes and plugins without modifying core files.

A separate premium version, sold as a one-time $199 lifetime purchase, adds newer social features including hashtags, video support, polls, events, stories, and link previews, extending the free core with capabilities common on modern commercial social platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OSSN's core script is completely free and open source, with no subscription fees or artificial user limits, and can be self-hosted on any PHP-compatible server or deployed via one-click marketplace listings on platforms like DigitalOcean.

Organizations wanting additional modern social features can purchase the premium version for a one-time fee of $199, which unlocks lifetime access to hashtags, video, polls, events, stories, and link previews on top of the free core.

features.title: User profiles and timelines
features.description: Chronological user profiles and activity timelines form the core of the social experience.

features.title: Groups, forums, and messaging
features.description: Members can create niche groups, participate in forums, and exchange private messages.

features.title: Photo albums and media sharing
features.description: Users can upload and share photo albums with likes and comments on shared content.

features.title: Hooks-and-events plugin architecture
features.description: Developers extend functionality with custom plugins and themes without editing core files.

features.title: Custom themes and responsive design
features.description: Site owners can restyle the platform with custom themes while retaining mobile responsiveness.

features.title: Full self-hosted data ownership
features.description: All user data and content stay on infrastructure the site owner controls, with no third-party platform dependency.

features.title: Premium modern social features
features.description: Paid add-on unlocking hashtags, video, polls, events, stories, and link previews for a one-time fee.

pros: Core platform is completely free and open source with no recurring subscription
Full data ownership and control since the site is self-hosted
One-time $199 fee for premium features avoids ongoing SaaS costs
Extensible hooks-and-events architecture supports custom plugins and themes
No artificial user or member limits imposed by the platform itself
Available as a one-click install via marketplaces like DigitalOcean and AWS
cons: Requires self-managed PHP hosting, server maintenance, and security updates
Modern social features like hashtags, video, and stories require the paid premium version
User interface is generally considered dated compared to modern SaaS community platforms
Smaller developer and plugin ecosystem than WordPress-based social alternatives like BuddyPress
Company/founder information behind the project is not prominently publicly disclosed

faqs.question: Is OSSN free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core OSSN script is free and open source with no subscription fees. A separate premium version is available for a one-time $199 lifetime fee that adds features like hashtags, video, and stories.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host OSSN?
faqs.answer: Yes, OSSN is self-hosted PHP software. You install it on your own server or hosting provider, though it is also available as a one-click app on marketplaces like DigitalOcean and AWS.

faqs.question: What features are in the OSSN premium version?
faqs.answer: The $199 one-time premium version adds hashtags, native video support, polls, events, stories, and link previews on top of the free core platform.

faqs.question: What does OSSN include for free?
faqs.answer: The free core includes user profiles, timelines, groups, forums, private messaging, photo albums, likes, comments, and notifications.

faqs.question: Can I customize OSSN with plugins?
faqs.answer: Yes, OSSN uses a hooks-and-events architecture that lets developers add custom plugins and themes without modifying the platform's core files.

faqs.question: Who owns the data on an OSSN site?
faqs.answer: Because OSSN is self-hosted, the site owner fully owns the codebase, branding, and all user data, with no dependency on a third-party social media provider.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to OSSN?
faqs.answer: Alternatives include Elgg, BuddyPress (for WordPress sites), Mastodon and other ActivityPub-based platforms, and hosted community platforms like Mighty Networks or Circle.

enrichment.short_description: osTicket is a widely used open-source customer support ticketing system maintained by Enhancesoft, offering a free self-hosted edition alongside paid cloud-hosted and enterprise plans.
enrichment.seo_title: osTicket Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: osTicket review 2026: the free open-source help desk ticketing system. Compare self-hosted, cloud and enterprise pricing, features and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: osTicket is an open-source customer support ticketing system that has been in continuous development since 2003, making it one of the longest-running open-source help desk platforms still in active use. It is maintained today by Enhancesoft, a software company headquartered in Alexandria, Louisiana, with an additional office in Eldoret, Kenya. osTicket's core value proposition is a mature, GPL-licensed, self-hosted ticketing system that organizations can run entirely for free, distinguishing it from most modern SaaS help desk tools that require an ongoing per-agent subscription. The platform converts emails, web forms, and API requests into trackable support tickets, and includes customizable ticket filters and routing rules, service level agreement (SLA) and business-hours management, configurable help topics and custom forms, department and agent management, and a customer-facing self-service portal with knowledge base articles. Enhancesoft monetizes osTicket through two paid tiers layered on top of the free open-source core: a Cloud-Hosted plan starting at $12 per agent per month, which includes managed infrastructure, daily backups, uptime guarantees, and email/phone support, and an Enterprise or Virtual Appliance tier with custom, quote-based pricing for organizations that need full customization alongside managed hosting. There is also SupportSystem, Enhancesoft's separately branded managed cloud offering built on the same osTicket codebase. osTicket's combination of a genuinely free, unlimited self-hosted edition and low-cost cloud entry pricing has made it a long-standing default choice for small and medium-sized businesses, IT help desks, and nonprofit organizations that need reliable ticketing without the cost of enterprise service-desk software. Its primary competitors include Zendesk and Freshdesk (both SaaS-first, more expensive help desk platforms), Zammad (a more modern open-source alternative), and OTRS/OTOBO (open-source ticketing systems oriented more toward ITSM than pure customer support). osTicket differentiates itself through its two-decade track record, large existing installed base, and the fact that its self-hosted edition remains completely free with no artificial agent limits, unlike many competitors that gate core functionality behind a paywall.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: osTicket is an open-source customer support ticketing system that has been actively developed since 2003, making it one of the most established open-source help desk platforms available today. It is maintained by Enhancesoft, a software company based in Alexandria, Louisiana.

The platform is available as a completely free, self-hosted, GPL-licensed edition, as well as paid Cloud-Hosted and Enterprise tiers for organizations that want managed infrastructure and additional support without giving up the underlying osTicket codebase.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: osTicket converts emails, web forms, and API submissions into trackable support tickets, with customizable filters and routing rules that direct tickets to the right department or agent automatically. SLA and business-hours management help teams enforce response-time policies.

The platform also includes a customer self-service portal with knowledge base articles, configurable help topics and custom forms, and department- and agent-level management, giving support teams the core building blocks of a full help desk without a mandatory subscription.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The self-hosted, open-source edition of osTicket is entirely free to download and run, with community-only support and no artificial limits on the number of agents.

The Cloud-Hosted plan starts at $12 per agent per month and includes managed infrastructure, daily backups, uptime guarantees, and email/phone support, while the Enterprise/Virtual Appliance tier offers custom pricing for organizations needing full customization alongside managed hosting.

features.description: Organize support staff into departments with role-based permissions and assignment rules.

pros: Self-hosted core edition is completely free with no per-agent licensing cost
Mature, battle-tested platform with over two decades of continuous development
Large community, documentation base, and plugin ecosystem
Flexible deployment as self-hosted, cloud-hosted, or enterprise virtual appliance
Granular SLA, routing, and department management for structured support teams
Affordable cloud entry price relative to major SaaS help desk competitors
cons: Self-hosted edition's interface feels dated compared to modern SaaS help desk tools
Cloud-Hosted plan is less customizable than the self-hosted edition
Advanced customization requires the higher-priced Enterprise/Virtual Appliance tier
Self-hosting requires ongoing server maintenance, updates, and security patching
Built-in reporting and analytics are more limited than newer competitors like Zendesk

faqs.question: Is osTicket really free?
faqs.answer: Yes. The self-hosted, open-source edition of osTicket is completely free to download and use, with no per-agent licensing fees. Paid Cloud-Hosted and Enterprise tiers are available for organizations that want managed infrastructure and support.

faqs.question: How much does osTicket Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: osTicket's Cloud-Hosted plan starts at $12 per agent per month and includes managed infrastructure, daily backups, uptime guarantees, and email/phone support, with a free trial available.

faqs.question: Who maintains osTicket?
faqs.answer: osTicket is maintained by Enhancesoft, a software company headquartered in Alexandria, Louisiana, with an additional office in Eldoret, Kenya.

faqs.question: How long has osTicket been around?
faqs.answer: osTicket has been in continuous development since 2003, making it one of the longest-running open-source help desk ticketing systems still actively maintained.

faqs.question: Can osTicket convert emails into tickets?
faqs.answer: Yes, osTicket supports multi-channel ticket intake, automatically converting emails, web form submissions, and API requests into trackable support tickets.

faqs.question: What is the difference between osTicket and SupportSystem?
faqs.answer: SupportSystem is Enhancesoft's separately branded, fully managed cloud version of osTicket, built on the same underlying codebase but delivered as a managed service rather than self-hosted software.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to osTicket?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Zendesk, Freshdesk, Zammad, and OTRS/OTOBO, though osTicket differentiates itself with a genuinely free, unlimited self-hosted edition.

enrichment.short_description: OTOBO is a free, open-source IT service management and ticketing platform forked from OTRS Community Edition in 2019, developed and commercially supported by Rother OSS GmbH in Germany.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted, unlimited agents); commercial support custom-quoted
enrichment.seo_title: OTOBO Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OTOBO review 2026: the free, open-source OTRS fork for IT service management and ticketing. Compare features, support pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OTOBO is an open-source IT service management (ITSM) and ticketing platform created in 2019 as a fork of OTRS Community Edition, built specifically to keep a fully free, GPLv3-licensed successor alive after OTRS AG signaled it would move away from a fully open community edition. OTOBO is developed and commercially backed by Rother OSS GmbH, a German software company headquartered in Leiblfing, Germany, founded by Stefan Rother, who was OTRS GmbH's first employee back in 2004 before leaving to start Rother OSS in 2011. Because Stefan Rother had deep firsthand experience with the original OTRS codebase, OTOBO was positioned from its founding as a way to return to the open-source roots of the ticketing system while adding modern capabilities, including Docker-based deployment, migration tooling from OTRS, Znuny, and the discontinued Community Edition, and a distinguishing AI-powered ticket classification plugin that uses machine learning to automatically pre-sort incoming tickets by category and priority. Functionally, OTOBO covers the full range of classic IT service desk operations: ticket recording and management, SLAs and escalation management, a knowledge base and self-service customer portal, process management workflows, and automation through OTOBO's Generic Agent engine, ticket templates, and auto-responses. The core software is completely free to self-host under the GPL v3 license, with no per-agent licensing fees and no cap on the number of agents, which is OTOBO's central differentiator versus commercial ticketing vendors including its own ancestor, OTRS. Rother OSS generates revenue not from software licensing but from professional services: consulting, training, hosting, technical support, and custom feature development for organizations that want expert help running OTOBO, priced on a custom, quote-based basis rather than published per-agent rates. OTOBO's target users are IT service management teams, managed service providers, and organizations that previously ran OTRS Community Edition and needed a free, actively maintained successor after OTRS AG discontinued that edition in December 2020. Its closest competitors and alternatives are OTRS itself (the commercial, proprietary-licensed original now majority-owned by EasyVista), Znuny (another community fork of OTRS Community Edition), Zammad, and osTicket, with OTOBO differentiating itself through its unlimited free agent model, AI ticket classification, and direct lineage to the original OTRS engineering team via founder Stefan Rother.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OTOBO is an open-source IT service management and ticketing platform created in 2019 as a fork of OTRS Community Edition, built to preserve a fully free, GPLv3-licensed alternative after OTRS AG moved away from supporting a fully open community edition. It is developed and commercially backed by Rother OSS GmbH, a German company founded by Stefan Rother, OTRS GmbH's first employee.

OTOBO covers classic IT service desk functionality, including ticket management, SLAs, and self-service portals, while adding modern capabilities like Docker-based deployment, migration tooling from OTRS and Znuny, and an AI-powered ticket classification plugin.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OTOBO's core ticketing engine handles ticket recording, SLA and escalation management, department routing, and a customer-facing self-service portal with a knowledge base. Automation is handled through the Generic Agent engine, ticket templates, and auto-responses.

A standout feature is OTOBO's AI ticket classification plugin, which uses machine learning to automatically pre-sort incoming tickets by category and priority, reducing manual triage work for support teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OTOBO's core software is completely free to self-host under the GPL v3 license, with unlimited agents and no per-agent licensing fees, making it one of the more cost-effective open-source ITSM platforms available.

Rother OSS monetizes through professional services rather than software licensing, offering consulting, training, managed hosting, technical support, and custom development on a custom, quote-based pricing basis.

pros: Core software is fully free with no per-agent licensing costs and unlimited agents
GPL v3 license guarantees the codebase stays fully open source
AI-powered ticket classification is a differentiated feature versus many open-source competitors
Built by a team with direct lineage to the original OTRS engineering organization
Active, independent development separate from the now EasyVista-owned OTRS AG
Migration tooling eases transition from discontinued OTRS Community Edition or Znuny
cons: Commercial support, hosting, and consulting pricing is not publicly listed and requires a custom quote
Smaller company (Rother OSS) than larger commercial ITSM vendors
Self-hosting requires familiarity with Perl and Docker-based deployment
User interface carries over some of the dated design inherited from the original OTRS codebase
Enterprise-grade support and advanced customization typically require paid consulting engagements

faqs.question: Is OTOBO really free?
faqs.answer: Yes. OTOBO's core software is free and open source under the GPL v3 license, with unlimited agents and no per-agent licensing fees when self-hosted. Paid options exist only for optional professional services like consulting, hosting, and support.

faqs.question: Who develops OTOBO?
faqs.answer: OTOBO is developed and commercially supported by Rother OSS GmbH, a German software company headquartered in Leiblfing, Germany, founded by Stefan Rother, who was previously OTRS GmbH's first employee.

faqs.question: How is OTOBO different from OTRS?
faqs.answer: OTOBO is a community fork of OTRS Community Edition created in 2019 to remain fully free and open source under GPL v3, while OTRS itself is now a commercial product from OTRS AG (majority-owned by EasyVista) with proprietary Business and Enterprise editions.

faqs.question: Can I migrate from OTRS to OTOBO?
faqs.answer: Yes, OTOBO includes built-in migration tooling to help organizations move from OTRS 7, the discontinued OTRS Community Edition, and Znuny.

faqs.question: Does OTOBO support AI ticket classification?
faqs.answer: Yes, OTOBO offers an AI-powered ticket classification plugin that uses machine learning to automatically pre-sort incoming tickets by category and priority.

faqs.question: How much does OTOBO support cost?
faqs.answer: OTOBO's core software is free, and professional support, hosting, and consulting from Rother OSS are priced on a custom, quote-based basis rather than published per-agent rates.

faqs.question: What license is OTOBO released under?
faqs.answer: OTOBO is licensed under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPL v3).

enrichment.short_description: OTRS is a commercial IT and customer service management platform from Germany's OTRS AG, offering ticketing, ITSM, and security incident response tools, after its free Community Edition was discontinued in December 2020.
enrichment.seo_title: OTRS Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OTRS review 2026: enterprise ticketing, ITSM and SOAR software from OTRS AG. Compare Business and Enterprise pricing, features and alternatives like OTOBO.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OTRS began in 2001 as an open-source help desk ticketing project created by developer Martin Edenhofer, with the commercial entity OTRS GmbH established in 2003 and later restructured as the publicly traded OTRS AG in 2007, listed on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange's Basic Board. The company is headquartered in Oberursel, Germany, and operates subsidiaries across the United States, Mexico, Singapore, Hong Kong, Brazil, and Hungary. Originally released under the GPL, OTRS progressively shifted toward a proprietary commercial model: the OTRS Business Solution launched as a paid add-on in 2015, and by 2018 the company offered a proprietary managed OTRS product and OTRS On-Premise alongside a still-free Community Edition. That changed in December 2020, when OTRS AG discontinued support for the free Community Edition entirely, a decision that prompted community members to create open-source forks, most notably Znuny and OTOBO, to preserve a free alternative; the Wikimedia Foundation, for example, migrated off the discontinued Community Edition to Znuny in April 2021. Today OTRS is sold exclusively as commercial software across Business and Enterprise editions, covering ticket and case management, IT service management (ITSM) capabilities, security incident response and SOAR (security orchestration, automation and response) tooling, SLA and escalation management, reporting dashboards, and information security management system (ISMS) features aligned with standards like ISO 27001. Pricing is quote-based, though third-party estimates place the Business Edition starting around $49 per agent per month, with Enterprise Edition pricing custom-quoted for large, complex deployments; broader market estimates suggest total costs in the range of roughly $25 to $100 per agent per month depending on modules. In December 2024, French service management vendor EasyVista acquired a majority stake in OTRS AG, folding OTRS into a larger enterprise service management portfolio. OTRS targets large enterprises, security operations centers, and regulated organizations that need deep ITSM and incident-response functionality with vendor accountability and formal support contracts, distinguishing it from lighter-weight open-source helpdesk tools. Its primary competitors and alternatives include ServiceNow (the dominant enterprise ITSM platform), Freshservice, Jira Service Management, and its own open-source descendants OTOBO and Znuny, both of which continue development of the codebase OTRS itself abandoned as free software in 2020.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OTRS began in 2001 as an open-source help desk ticketing project created by developer Martin Edenhofer, with the commercial company OTRS GmbH founded in 2003 and later becoming the publicly traded OTRS AG, headquartered in Oberursel, Germany. OTRS AG operates subsidiaries in the United States, Mexico, Singapore, Hong Kong, Brazil, and Hungary.

The company progressively moved from a free open-source model toward a commercial one, culminating in the discontinuation of its free Community Edition in December 2020. That decision prompted the community to create open-source forks, most notably OTOBO and Znuny, to continue a free version of the codebase.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OTRS today is positioned as an enterprise service management platform covering ticket and case management, IT service management (ITSM), and security incident response with SOAR (security orchestration, automation and response) capabilities, alongside SLA and escalation management and reporting dashboards.

The platform also includes information security management system (ISMS) tooling aligned with standards like ISO 27001, supporting regulated organizations that need formal compliance documentation alongside their service desk operations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OTRS pricing is quote-based rather than published outright, with the Business Edition estimated to start around $49 per agent per month according to third-party sources, and total costs for complex deployments estimated in the $25 to $100 per agent per month range depending on selected modules.

The Enterprise Edition, aimed at large organizations with complex requirements, is priced entirely on a custom quote basis. There is no longer a free Community Edition available, as OTRS AG discontinued it in December 2020.

features.description: Flexible deployment options including on-premise installation and vendor-managed hosting.

pros: Mature, enterprise-grade platform with over two decades of development history
Strong depth in ITSM and SOAR functionality beyond basic ticketing
Publicly traded parent company (OTRS AG) provides financial transparency and accountability
International subsidiary network supports large, multi-region enterprise deployments
ISMS/compliance tooling suited to regulated industries
Formal vendor support contracts and SLAs available for enterprise customers
cons: Free Community Edition was discontinued in December 2020, removing the no-cost entry option
Pricing is quote-based and can be costly at scale compared to open-source alternatives
Steeper learning curve and implementation complexity than lightweight helpdesk tools
Organizations must migrate to a paid edition or an open-source fork like OTOBO or Znuny
Smaller open-source community today since the shift to a fully commercial model

faqs.question: Is OTRS free to use?
faqs.answer: No. OTRS AG discontinued the free Community Edition in December 2020, and OTRS is now sold exclusively as commercial software through Business and Enterprise editions.

faqs.question: How much does OTRS cost?
faqs.answer: OTRS pricing is quote-based. Third-party estimates put the Business Edition starting around $49 per agent per month, with total costs commonly estimated between $25 and $100 per agent per month depending on modules, and Enterprise Edition pricing fully custom.

faqs.question: What is the difference between OTRS and OTOBO?
faqs.answer: OTRS is the commercial, proprietary-licensed original product from OTRS AG in Germany. OTOBO is an independent open-source fork of the former OTRS Community Edition, created in 2019 by Rother OSS GmbH, and remains free and GPL v3 licensed.

faqs.question: When was OTRS founded?
faqs.answer: The OTRS project began in 2001, created by developer Martin Edenhofer, with the commercial company OTRS GmbH founded in 2003 and later restructured as the publicly traded OTRS AG in 2007.

faqs.question: Where is OTRS AG headquartered?
faqs.answer: OTRS AG is headquartered in Oberursel, Germany, with subsidiaries in the United States, Mexico, Singapore, Hong Kong, Brazil, and Hungary.

faqs.question: Who owns OTRS AG today?
faqs.answer: In December 2024, French enterprise service management vendor EasyVista acquired a majority stake in OTRS AG.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to OTRS?
faqs.answer: Alternatives include ServiceNow, Freshservice, Jira Service Management, and the open-source forks OTOBO and Znuny, which continue development of the discontinued OTRS Community Edition codebase.

enrichment.short_description: OTS (One Time Secrets, hosted at ots.fyi) is a free, open-source, self-hosted tool for sharing sensitive text or files as self-destructing links, encrypting secrets with AES-256 in the browser before they reach the server.
enrichment.company_size: Not publicly disclosed (independently maintained open-source project)
enrichment.seo_title: OTS Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OTS (ots.fyi) review 2026: free, open-source, self-hosted one-time secret sharing with browser-side AES-256 encryption. Features, setup and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OTS, short for One Time Secrets and publicly demoed at ots.fyi, is a free, open-source, self-hosted tool for sharing sensitive text such as passwords, API keys, or private notes as one-time, self-destructing links. It is developed and maintained by an individual open-source developer known as Luzifer, whose GitHub repository (Luzifer/ots) hosts the project under the Apache 2.0 license, distinguishing OTS from venture-backed commercial secret-sharing products since there is no company, funding round, or enterprise sales team behind it. The core technical design is what sets OTS apart: rather than sending a plaintext secret to a server for storage, the secret is encrypted client-side in the browser using symmetric 256-bit AES encryption before transmission, and the decryption password is embedded only in the URL fragment (the part after the # symbol), which browsers never send to the server. As a result, the server only ever sees ciphertext and does not have practical means to decrypt stored secrets, and once a secret has been read for the first time, it is immediately and permanently deleted from storage, preventing replay or later retrieval even by someone with server access. Beyond the web interface, OTS ships a command-line interface for creating and retrieving secrets programmatically, with configurable expiry times and the ability to attach files, making it usable in scripting and automation workflows as well as ad hoc sharing. For deployment, OTS is distributed as a Docker image (ghcr.io/luzifer/ots) and as standalone binaries, and it supports pluggable storage backends including in-memory storage for lightweight, ephemeral deployments and Redis for more durable, production-grade self-hosted instances; ots.fyi itself serves as the project's official public demo instance, free for anyone to use without registration. Because OTS has no company or funding behind it, there is no pricing model beyond 'free and self-hosted'; there are no paid tiers, enterprise contracts, or managed hosting options offered by the maintainer. OTS's target users are developers, sysadmins, and IT teams who need to share credentials or sensitive configuration values securely with colleagues or clients, as well as privacy-conscious individuals who want to avoid pasting secrets into email, Slack, or chat tools where they persist indefinitely. Its closest alternatives include Onetime Secret (onetimesecret.com and its open-source codebase, the most widely known product in this category and a likely namesake influence), PrivateBin (a similarly self-hosted, browser-encrypted pastebin-style tool), and Yopass, with OTS differentiating itself through its lightweight Go and Vue codebase, simple Docker-based self-hosting, and companion CLI tool for scripted secret sharing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OTS, or One Time Secrets, is a free, open-source tool for sharing sensitive text and files as self-destructing, one-time-use links. The public demo instance runs at ots.fyi, and the underlying project is developed and maintained by independent open-source developer Luzifer under the Apache 2.0 license.

Unlike commercial secret-sharing products, OTS has no company or funding behind it; it is a lightweight, community-maintained tool intended to be self-hosted by individuals, development teams, or IT departments who want full control over how sensitive text is shared and stored.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OTS encrypts secrets with symmetric 256-bit AES encryption directly in the browser before the data is ever sent to the server, and the decryption password lives only in the URL fragment, which browsers do not transmit to the server. This means the server only ever stores and handles ciphertext.

Secrets are permanently deleted immediately after their first read, preventing later retrieval even by someone with direct server access. A companion command-line interface allows scripted creation and retrieval of secrets with configurable expiry times and file attachments, and the project supports both in-memory and Redis storage backends for self-hosted deployments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OTS is entirely free and open source, with no company, subscription plans, or paid tiers behind it. Self-hosters only bear the cost of running their own server or container, such as via the official Docker image.
The public ots.fyi instance is also free to use directly for occasional secret sharing without any registration or account, making it accessible without self-hosting for lighter use cases.

features.title: Client-side AES-256 encryption
features.description: Secrets are encrypted in the browser using symmetric 256-bit AES encryption before being sent to the server.

features.title: Self-destructing secrets
features.description: Secrets are permanently deleted immediately after their first successful read.

features.title: Server-blind password handling
features.description: The decryption password is embedded only in the URL fragment, which browsers never transmit to the server.

features.title: Command-line interface
features.description: A CLI tool allows scripted creation and retrieval of secrets, useful for automation workflows.

features.title: Configurable expiry and file attachments
features.description: Secrets can be set to expire after a defined time window and can include attached files.

features.title: Pluggable storage backends
features.description: Supports in-memory storage for lightweight setups or Redis for more durable, production-grade deployments.

features.title: Docker-based self-hosting
features.description: Distributed as a Docker image and standalone binaries for straightforward self-hosted deployment.

pros: Completely free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license
Strong client-side encryption model means the server never sees plaintext secrets
Self-destructing links prevent secrets from being retrieved more than once
Lightweight Docker-based self-hosting with minimal setup
Command-line interface supports scripted, automated secret sharing
Public ots.fyi demo instance is usable without registration for quick one-off sharing
cons: No official managed or enterprise hosting option since there is no company behind the project
Maintained by an individual developer rather than a dedicated team, which can affect long-term support guarantees
Lacks enterprise features like SSO, audit logs, or access controls found in commercial secret-management tools
Minimal, utilitarian interface compared to polished commercial secret-sharing products
Self-hosting requires basic server and Docker administration knowledge

faqs.question: Is OTS (ots.fyi) free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, OTS is completely free and open source. The public demo at ots.fyi can be used directly at no cost, and the underlying software can be self-hosted for free as well.

faqs.question: Who maintains OTS?
faqs.answer: OTS is developed and maintained by an independent open-source developer known as Luzifer, with the source code hosted on GitHub under the Apache 2.0 license, rather than by a commercial company.

faqs.question: How does OTS keep secrets secure?
faqs.answer: OTS encrypts secrets with symmetric 256-bit AES encryption directly in the browser before sending them to the server, and the decryption password is embedded only in the URL fragment, which is never transmitted to the server, so the server only handles ciphertext.

faqs.question: What happens after a secret is read on OTS?
faqs.answer: The secret is immediately and permanently deleted from storage after its first successful read, preventing it from being retrieved again.

faqs.question: Can I self-host OTS instead of using ots.fyi?
faqs.answer: Yes, OTS is distributed as a Docker image and standalone binaries, with support for in-memory or Redis storage backends, making it straightforward to self-host on your own infrastructure.

faqs.question: Does OTS have a command-line tool?
faqs.answer: Yes, OTS includes a command-line interface for creating and retrieving secrets programmatically, with support for configurable expiry times and file attachments.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to OTS?
faqs.answer: Alternatives include Onetime Secret, PrivateBin, and Yopass, all of which offer similar self-hosted or hosted one-time secret sharing with client-side encryption.

use_cases.title: Secure credential sharing between developers
use_cases.description: Developers use OTS to share passwords, API keys, and tokens with teammates as self-destructing links instead of pasting them into chat or email.

use_cases.title: IT password delivery to end users
use_cases.description: IT teams send temporary account passwords to employees or clients as one-time links that disappear after being viewed.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious personal secret sharing
use_cases.description: Individuals use the public ots.fyi instance or a self-hosted copy to share sensitive personal information without leaving a persistent copy in email or chat history.
